summary: Patrick McKenzie interviews Thomas Smale of FE International about the process of buying and selling online businesses, drawing on Patrick's experience selling Bingo Card Creator through the brokerage. The conversation covers how SaaS businesses command a ~25% valuation premium over other online businesses due to recurring revenue predictability, with typical multiples of 3-5x annual seller discretionary earnings. They walk through the full acquisition process from initial consultation through prospectus creation, buyer matching, due diligence, contract negotiation, and handover. Patrick emphasizes the importance of preparing months in advance by separating shared infrastructure, documenting code, and keeping financials clean. The episode also reveals that many small SaaS buyers are non-technical, and that working with a reputable broker provides critical value when deals hit unexpected snags.
key_points: SaaS businesses sell at roughly 3x projected next-year earnings or 5x trailing twelve months, with a ~25% premium over content or e-commerce businesses due to recurring revenue stability
Sellers should begin preparing months before a sale by separating shared infrastructure, documenting code, getting financials in order, and having a third party review the codebase
Many buyers of small SaaS businesses ($100-200k range) are non-technical, making clean documentation and available freelancer support essential for closing deals

Continuing to run the business normally during the sales process is critical — declining metrics significantly reduce multiples, but artificially juicing numbers (like discounted annual plans) is equally damaging
Using a reputable broker provides value through structured processes, backup buyer pipelines, deal recovery when things go wrong, and standardized contract terms that protect both parties
money_quotes: The real driver there is the fact that a SaaS business has that recurring income and that baseline. From a buyer's perspective, worst case scenario, even if you can't grow the business or even if it starts to decline, you've still got that baseline of subscribers.
It always amazes me how many small business owners run businesses and they have no idea what they've got going in or out each month.
No one wants to buy a business on a decline. Businesses that are declining are going to attract a significantly lower multiple than either stable or a growing business.
Did you know it costs $200k to open a Subway or $750k in capital to open a McDonalds? The Internet is enabling a disruptively better path towards yeoman entrepreneurship.
Your $1 million offer might actually be better than the $1.2 million offer that has slightly worse terms attached to it, and a buyer that you don't necessarily think is going to be a good fit.

Many of my SaaS friends who were using legacy payment processors end up using natural churn to transition accounts between processors, since legacy processors hate doing this. This process can take years and is unimaginably frustrating.

You shouldn't really be entering into a transaction if you don't trust the other party. It should very much be fundamentally built on trust and goodwill, and ultimately two parties who actually want to deal with each other.
key_insight: Selling a small online business successfully requires months of unglamorous preparation — separating infrastructure, documenting code, cleaning financials — and a structured process with a trusted broker who maintains backup buyers and standardized terms to navigate the inevitable complications.
